Alamut Castle falls to the Mongols. Tusi enters the service of the Mongol ruler Hulagu Khan.
Construction begins on the Maragheh observatory under Tusi's direction.
Completes his major work on ethics, Akhlaq-i Nasiri (The Nasirean Ethics).
The Maragheh observatory team completes the highly influential astronomical tables, the Zij-i Ilkhani.
Dies in Kadhimiya, near Baghdad, and is buried in a shrine next to the seventh Twelver Shia Imam.
Maragheh Observatory
Founded and directed the most advanced astronomical observatory of its time, attracting top scholars from across the known world.
The Tusi Couple
A mathematical theorem that generates linear motion from the sum of two circular motions, which was later used by Copernicus.
Zij-i Ilkhani (Ilkhanic Tables)
A highly accurate set of astronomical tables that charted planetary movements, becoming a standard reference for centuries.
Akhlaq-i Nasiri (Nasirean Ethics)
A seminal work on ethics that synthesized Aristotelian, Platonic, and Islamic thought, influencing Islamic philosophy for generations.
Was Tusi really a prisoner of the Assassins?

He initially sought refuge with them but was later prevented from leaving, effectively making him a prisoner. However, he was treated as a scholar, not a common captive, and was able to continue his scientific and philosophical work.
How did Tusi influence Copernicus?

Tusi's mathematical model, the 'Tusi couple,' appears in Copernicus's major work, De revolutionibus. It is used to replace Ptolemy's equant. It is debated whether Copernicus learned of it through Byzantine or Arabic manuscripts, but the mathematical similarity is undeniable.
What fields did Tusi contribute to?

He was a true polymath who made significant contributions to astronomy, mathematics (especially trigonometry), philosophy, ethics, logic, optics, medicine, and theology. He wrote over 150 works in his lifetime.
What was his role in the Mongol invasion?

When the Mongols besieged the Assassin fortress of Alamut, Tusi helped negotiate a peaceful surrender. His knowledge and wisdom impressed the Mongol leader Hulagu Khan, who then appointed him as a scientific advisor, saving his life and enabling his greatest achievements.
c. 634 BCE
Birth of Nebuchadnezzar II.
605 BCE
Defeats Egyptians at the Battle of Carchemish; ascends to the throne of Babylon.
597 BCE
First siege of Jerusalem. Deports King Jehoiachin and other elites to Babylon.
Second siege of Jerusalem. Destroys the city and Solomon's Temple, beginning the Babylonian Captivity.
Conducts a 13-year siege of the city of Tyre.
Reigns as king, overseeing massive construction projects in Babylon, including the Ishtar Gate.
c. 562 BCE
Dies in Babylon, succeeded by his son Amel-Marduk.
Conquest of Judah and Destruction of Jerusalem
He crushed the Kingdom of Judah, destroying Solomon's Temple and deporting its populace, a pivotal event in Jewish history.
Rebuilding of Babylon
He transformed Babylon into the largest and most magnificent city of its time, constructing the Ishtar Gate and the Etemenanki ziggurat.
Imperial Expansion
He secured the Neo-Babylonian Empire's dominance over the Near East after defeating Egyptian and Assyrian forces at Carchemish.
Did Nebuchadnezzar really build the Hanging Gardens of Babylon?

While ancient writers like Berossus credit him with building the Hanging Gardens for his wife, there is no definitive archaeological evidence or contemporary Babylonian text that confirms their existence. Their story remains a legendary part of his reign.
Why is he so prominent in the Bible?

He is a central antagonist in the Old Testament for conquering Jerusalem, destroying the First Temple, and forcing the Jews into the Babylonian Captivity. The Book of Daniel also features him prominently, detailing his interactions with the prophet Daniel and his supposed period of madness.
What was his relationship with the god Marduk?

As king of Babylon, Nebuchadnezzar was the chief servant of Marduk, the patron god of the city. His inscriptions frequently credit Marduk for his victories and describe his extensive temple-building projects as acts of piety to honor the god.
Born Abu Ali Hasan ibn Ali Tusi in Tus, Persia.
Appointed as vizier to the Seljuk Sultan Alp Arslan.
Secures the throne for Malik-Shah I after Alp Arslan's assassination, becoming the empire's most powerful figure.
Founds the Nizamiyyah schools across the empire, with the most famous one in Baghdad established in 1065.
Writes the Siyasatnama (Book of Government), a major work on statecraft.
Assassinated near Nahavand, Persia, believed to be by a member of the Order of Assassins.
Centralization of the Seljuk Empire
He created a powerful centralized bureaucracy that unified the vast and diverse territories of the Seljuk Empire.
Founder of the Nizamiyyahs
He established a network of state-sponsored universities (Nizamiyyahs) that became premier institutions of learning and administration.
Author of Siyasatnama
He wrote the 'Book of Government' (Siyasatnama), a foundational text on political theory and statecraft in the Islamic world.
What were the Nizamiyyah schools?
They were a system of higher education institutions founded by Nizam al-Mulk. They promoted Sunni Islamic theology and law, trained future government officials, and countered rival ideologies.
Why was Nizam al-Mulk assassinated?

While the Order of Assassins (Hashshashin) is officially blamed, his immense power had created many enemies at court, including rival ministers and members of the royal family, who may have been complicit.
What is the 'Siyasatnama' about?

The 'Siyasatnama' or 'Book of Government' is a treatise on kingship and statecraft. It offers advice to rulers on justice, administration, court etiquette, and dealing with internal and external threats.
c. 550 BCE
Birth of Darius.
522 BCE
Seizes the throne of the Achaemenid Empire after overthrowing the usurper Gaumata.
Suppresses widespread revolts across the empire.
c. 519 BCE
Begins work on the Behistun Inscription to legitimize his rule.
c. 515 BCE
Begins construction of the new ceremonial capital, Persepolis.
c. 513 BCE
Launches an unsuccessful campaign against the Scythians in Europe.
499 BCE
The Ionian Revolt begins, marking the start of the Greco-Persian Wars.
490 BCE
The first Persian invasion of Greece ends in defeat at the Battle of Marathon.
486 BCE
Dies in Persia while preparing a second expedition against Greece.
Imperial Reorganization (Satrapy System)
Divided the empire into provinces (satrapies), each governed by officials who reported directly to him, ensuring centralized control.
The Royal Road
Constructed a 2,700 km highway from Susa to Sardis, enabling rapid communication and troop movement via a relay of postal stations.
Standardized Coinage and Weights
Introduced the gold daric and silver siglos, creating a unified economic system across the vast empire.
Construction of Persepolis
Initiated the building of the grand ceremonial capital, showcasing the wealth, power, and multicultural nature of the Achaemenid Empire.
Was Darius related to Cyrus the Great?
Distantly. Darius belonged to a collateral branch of the Achaemenid dynasty. He solidified his claim by marrying Atossa, the daughter of Cyrus the Great.
What was the Behistun Inscription?
A massive multilingual inscription carved on a cliff, detailing Darius's rise to power. It was a key piece of royal propaganda and was crucial for the modern decipherment of cuneiform script.
Did Darius conquer Greece?
No. His first invasion was famously defeated at the Battle of Marathon in 490 BCE. He died before he could launch a second, much larger invasion, which was left to his son, Xerxes I.
Born in al-Qalamoun, near Tripoli, in Ottoman Syria.
Moved to Cairo, Egypt, to collaborate with Muhammad Abduh.
Founded the influential journal Al-Manar (The Lighthouse).
Following Abduh's death, he became the leading voice of the Islamic Modernist movement.
Founded the 'Society for Preaching and Guidance' in Cairo.
Elected President of the Syrian National Congress.
The abolition of the Ottoman Caliphate deeply impacted his later political thought.
Died in Cairo, Egypt.
Publication of Al-Manar
Published the influential journal Al-Manar for over 35 years, spreading Islamic reformist ideas throughout the Muslim world.
Tafsir al-Manar
Authored a monumental commentary on the Quran, Tafsir al-Manar, continuing the work started by Muhammad Abduh.
Bridging Modernism and Salafism
Synthesized the modernist ideas of Abduh with the principles of Salafism, creating a unique and influential intellectual current.
What is Salafism, as Rida understood it?

For Rida, Salafism meant returning to the practices and beliefs of the 'pious predecessors' (the Salaf al-Salih), the first three generations of Muslims, as the purest form of Islam, to reform the religion from later accretions and superstitions.
How did his views on the Caliphate change?

Initially a supporter of the Ottoman Caliphate, its abolition in 1924 led him to theorize about a new form of Islamic state, an 'Islamic Caliphate' based on consultation (shura) and led by a qualified ruler, which heavily influenced later Islamist thought.
What was his relationship with Arab nationalism?

It was complex. He supported Arab autonomy from the Ottoman Turks and presided over the Syrian National Congress, but his ultimate loyalty was to the unity of the Islamic Ummah (community) over ethnic nationalism.
Reza Khan is born in Alasht, Mazandaran Province, Persia.
Leads a coup d'état with the Persian Cossack Brigade, becoming Minister of War.
Is voted Shah by the Majlis (parliament), ending the Qajar dynasty and founding the Pahlavi dynasty.
Officially requests foreign nations to use the name 'Iran' instead of 'Persia' in formal correspondence.
Establishes the University of Tehran, a cornerstone of his educational reforms.
Issues the 'Kashf-e hijab' decree, controversially banning all veils in public.
Is forced to abdicate by the Anglo-Soviet invasion of Iran during World War II.
Dies in exile in Johannesburg, South Africa.
Founding the Pahlavi Dynasty
He overthrew the Qajar dynasty and established his own, ruling as the first Shah of the Pahlavi line from 1925 to 1941.
Nation-Building and Modernization
He initiated large-scale industrial projects, built national infrastructure like the Trans-Iranian Railway, and reformed the military, judiciary, and education systems.
Secularization and Westernization
He implemented policies to reduce the power of the clergy, promoted secular education, and enforced Western-style dress codes to modernize society.
Was Reza Shah a king?
Yes, he was the Shah of Iran. 'Shah' is the Persian title for a king or emperor.
Why did he change the country's name from Persia to Iran?
In 1935, he requested that foreign nations use the name 'Iran', the country's native name, to signify a break from the past and assert a modern national identity on the world stage.
What was his relationship with the Allied and Axis powers in WWII?

He declared Iran's neutrality in WWII but was suspected by the Allies of sympathizing with Nazi Germany. This suspicion, combined with Iran's strategic importance, led to the Anglo-Soviet invasion in 1941 and his forced abdication.
Born in Mardinu, Babylonia, within the Parthian Empire.
Receives his first revelation from his spiritual twin, the Syzygos.
Receives his second revelation, commanding him to begin his public ministry.
Travels to the Kushan Empire in modern-day Afghanistan and India, encountering Buddhist thought.
Returns to Persia and is granted an audience with the newly crowned Sasanian Emperor, Shapur I, gaining his favor.
Preaches and spreads his religion throughout the Sasanian Empire and beyond under royal protection.
Accused of heresy by the Zoroastrian clergy, he is imprisoned and executed by order of Emperor Bahram I.
Founding of Manichaeism
Created a major gnostic world religion that synthesized Zoroastrian, Christian, and Buddhist elements and spread from Rome to China.
Author of a New Scripture
Authored several holy books, including the Shabuhragan, which were famously and beautifully illustrated to convey his message.
Universal Missionary Vision
Organized a structured church with a clear missionary goal to create a single, universal religion for all humanity, transcending cultural and ethnic boundaries.
What is the core belief of Manichaeism?
Manichaeism is a radically dualistic religion believing the universe is a battleground between a good, spiritual world of Light and an evil, material world of Darkness.
Why did Manichaeism eventually disappear?

It faced intense and sustained persecution from competing state religions like Zoroastrianism, Roman Christianity, and later, Islam, leading to its gradual decline and eventual extinction by the 16th century.
Was Mani a painter?
Yes, Mani was a renowned artist and scribe. He believed art was a crucial medium for conveying his divine message, and Manichaean manuscripts were famously beautiful and highly valued.
What does the term 'Manichaean' mean today?
Today, the adjective 'Manichaean' is often used more broadly to describe any worldview that sees things in a simplistic, dualistic way, as purely good versus purely evil.
Ascends to the throne after the overthrow of his brother, Mustafa IV.
The 'Auspicious Incident' (Vaka-i Hayriye), where he abolishes the Janissary corps.
The Treaty of Adrianople concludes the Russo-Turkish War, granting autonomy to Serbia and Greece.
Establishes the first official Ottoman newspaper, Takvim-i Vekayi (Calendar of Events).
Dies of tuberculosis, shortly before his planned Edict of Gülhane (part of the Tanzimat) is proclaimed.
Abolition of the Janissaries
Eliminated the powerful, conservative military corps that had long obstructed modernization and controlled politics.
Creation of a Modern Army
Established the 'Asakir-i Mansure-i Muhammediye' (The Victorious Soldiers of Muhammad), a European-style conscript army.
Administrative Centralization
Replaced traditional advisory councils (Divan) with European-style ministries to centralize state power.
Foundation of Tanzimat
Initiated the legal and social reforms that would be codified in the Tanzimat Edict, promoting equality among Ottoman subjects.
Why were the Janissaries so opposed to reform?
The Janissaries had evolved from an elite fighting force into a deeply entrenched political and economic power, and they saw reforms as a direct threat to their privileges and influence.
What does 'Tanzimat' mean?
'Tanzimat' is Turkish for 'reorganization.' It refers to a period of intense reform in the Ottoman Empire from 1839 to 1876, which Mahmud II's reign set in motion.
What was the 'Auspicious Incident'?

It was the event in 1826 where Mahmud II used his loyal artillery to crush a Janissary revolt, leading to the corps' complete destruction and abolition, which removed the main obstacle to his reforms.
Did Mahmud II's reforms succeed?

They were a mixed success. While he laid crucial groundwork for modernizing the state and military, the empire continued to face territorial losses and internal strife, and the reforms were often met with resistance.
Born in Edirne, the Ottoman capital at the time.
First ascends the throne at age 12 after his father's abdication, but is deposed in favor of his father due to court intrigue and external threats.
Ascends the throne for the second time upon the death of his father, Murad II.
Conquers Constantinople after a 53-day siege, effectively ending the Byzantine Empire and making it the new Ottoman capital, Istanbul.
Conquers the Empire of Trebizond, the last major Byzantine successor state.
Wages a long and costly war with the Republic of Venice for control of the Aegean and Adriatic seas.
Compiles the 'Kanunname,' a code of laws that centralized power and shaped the structure of the Ottoman state for centuries.
Dies suddenly during a new military campaign, with the cause of death remaining a subject of historical debate (poison or natural causes).
Conquest of Constantinople (1453)
Ended the 1,100-year-old Byzantine Empire and established Istanbul as the new, prosperous Ottoman capital.
Conquered vast territories in Anatolia and the Balkans (including Serbia, Bosnia, and Albania), transforming the Ottoman state into a dominant world empire.
Legal and Administrative Reforms
Issued the 'Kanunname,' a secular legal code that centralized power and defined the structure of the Ottoman government and society.
Patronage of Arts and Science
Established universities, invited Italian and Greek scholars to his court, and fostered a vibrant intellectual and artistic culture in Istanbul.
Why was conquering Constantinople so important?

It was a huge symbolic victory, the fulfillment of a long-held Islamic ambition, and it gave the Ottomans strategic control over crucial trade routes between Europe and Asia, solidifying their status as a world power.
Was he tolerant of other religions?

He showed a degree of pragmatism. After the conquest, he allowed the Greek Orthodox, Armenian, and Jewish communities to maintain their own religious laws and institutions under the Millet system, though non-Muslims were subject to special taxes.

The exact cause of his death in 1481 is debated. While on a campaign, he suddenly fell ill and died. Some historians suggest he was poisoned, possibly by his eldest son Bayezid or by Venetian agents, while others believe he died of natural causes like gout.
Born in Salonica (now Thessaloniki, Greece), Ottoman Empire.
Commands Ottoman forces to victory at the Battle of Gallipoli, gaining national fame.
Initiates the Turkish War of Independence against occupying Allied Powers.
Establishes the Grand National Assembly of Turkey in Ankara.
Abolishes the Ottoman Sultanate.
Proclaims the Republic of Turkey and is elected as its first President.
Initiates the replacement of the Arabic-based Ottoman Turkish script with the Latin-based Turkish alphabet.
Grants full universal suffrage to women and is given the surname 'Atatürk' (Father of the Turks) by the parliament.
Dies in Dolmabahçe Palace, Istanbul, at the age of 57.
Turkish War of Independence
He led the Turkish National Movement to victory against Allied occupation, securing the sovereignty of modern Turkey.
Founding the Republic of Turkey
He abolished the 600-year-old Ottoman Sultanate and established a modern, secular republic in its place.
Atatürk's Reforms (Kemalism)
He implemented a series of sweeping reforms in politics, law, language, education, and culture to create a modern nation-state.
Women's Suffrage
He championed women's rights, granting Turkish women universal suffrage in 1934, earlier than in many Western nations.
What does the name 'Atatürk' mean?
It means 'Father of the Turks.' The Turkish Parliament bestowed this surname upon him in 1934 with the 'Surname Law,' which required all citizens to adopt a hereditary surname.
Why did he replace the Arabic script with the Latin alphabet?

The reform was aimed at increasing literacy, as the new alphabet was easier to learn and better suited to the sounds of the Turkish language. It was also a symbolic move to reorient Turkey culturally towards the West and away from its Ottoman and Islamic past.
Was Atatürk a dictator?

His rule is complex. He governed a single-party state and wielded immense authority to push through rapid, top-down reforms, which some view as authoritarian. However, his stated goal was to establish the foundations for a future democracy, and he is revered in Turkey as the nation's founder.
What was his view on religion?

He was a staunch secularist. He believed that religion was a private matter and should be completely separate from public life, politics, and law. This principle, laïcité, became a cornerstone of the Turkish Republic.
Born in Kavala, Ottoman Empire (now in Greece).
Arrives in Egypt as second-in-command of an Albanian contingent of the Ottoman army.
Appointed Wāli (governor) of Egypt by the Ottoman Sultan after popular demand.
Consolidates power by massacring the Mamluk leaders at the Citadel in Cairo.
Wages war against the Wahhabis in Arabia on behalf of the Sultan, extending his influence.
Conquers Sudan, seeking gold, slaves, and recruits for his army.
Intervenes in the Greek War of Independence, but his navy is destroyed at the Battle of Navarino.
First Egyptian-Ottoman War. His son Ibrahim Pasha conquers Syria and advances into Anatolia.
Second Egyptian-Ottoman War. European intervention forces him to withdraw from Syria but grants his family hereditary rule of Egypt.
Steps down from power due to failing health.
Dies in Alexandria.
Founder of Modern Egypt
Established a dynasty and implemented sweeping reforms that modernized Egypt's military, economy, and society.
Created a formidable, European-style conscript army and a modern navy, making Egypt a major regional military power.
Economic Centralization
Established state monopolies over agriculture and trade, particularly in cotton, to fund his ambitious projects.
Educational and Administrative Reforms
Sent educational missions to Europe and established a centralized bureaucracy to govern the state effectively.
Was Muhammad Ali Egyptian?

No, he was an ethnic Albanian, born in Kavala in modern-day Greece, which was then part of the Ottoman Empire. He came to Egypt as an Ottoman soldier and reportedly never learned to speak Arabic fluently.
Why is he called the 'founder of modern Egypt'?

His reforms fundamentally transformed Egypt from a neglected Ottoman province into a dynamic, quasi-independent state with a powerful military, a centralized government, and a modernizing economy, laying the groundwork for the modern nation-state.
Did he want to overthrow the Ottoman Sultan?

While he fought two major wars against the Sultan and sought to create a vast personal empire, his ultimate goal was likely to secure hereditary rule for his family and establish a powerful, independent state centered in Egypt, rather than to take the imperial throne in Istanbul himself.
Born in a village in Lower Egypt.
Enrolled at Al-Azhar University in Cairo.
Met his influential mentor, Jamal al-Din al-Afghani.
Exiled from Egypt for his support of the 'Urabi Revolt.
Co-founded the pan-Islamic journal 'Al-Urwah al-Wuthqa' in Paris.
Returned to Egypt from exile.
Appointed Grand Mufti of Egypt, the nation's highest authority on Islamic law.
Died in Alexandria, Egypt.
Founding Islamic Modernism
He articulated a framework for reinterpreting Islamic principles in light of modern challenges, emphasizing reason and rationality.
Educational Reform
He advocated for a modernized curriculum in religious schools, including secular sciences, to create a well-rounded generation of leaders.
Legal Reform as Grand Mufti
He issued progressive fatwas (legal opinions) on topics like banking interest and the status of women, using his authority to enact change.
What was Muhammad Abduh's main goal?

His main goal was to reform Islamic thought and institutions to prove that Islam was compatible with modernity, reason, and science, thereby revitalizing Muslim societies under the pressure of colonialism.
He was exiled for his connection to the nationalist 'Urabi Revolt (1881-1882), which sought to end British and French influence over Egypt.
What is the difference between Abduh and his mentor al-Afghani?

While both were reformers, al-Afghani was a more radical pan-Islamist political activist focused on anti-colonial revolution. After his exile, Abduh shifted his focus to gradual, long-term change through education and legal reform from within the system.
Born in the village of Ağırnas near Kayseri, Anatolia.
Recruited into the Ottoman Janissary corps through the Devşirme system.
Appointed as the Chief Imperial Architect by Sultan Suleiman the Magnificent.
